summary: DHH highlights the fundamental tradeoff between scope, resources, quality, and time in software development. He argues that project managers routinely violate these constraints by demanding shorter timelines without adjusting scope, budget, or quality expectations. The post references the classic 'iron triangle' of project management. He points readers to an Extreme Programming parable ('The King's Dinner') that illustrates why you can't compress timelines without consequences.
key_points: Scope, resources, quality, and time are interconnected — changing one necessarily affects the others
Project managers frequently demand compressed timelines while holding all other variables fixed
The expectation to 'just work harder' is a denial of fundamental project constraints
money_quotes: Unaware of the laws of nature, plenty project managers decide to change one of the four basic development aspects without recognizing the effect it will have on one or more of the remaining aspects.
Five weeks, you say? Give me the same in three (just work harder)! And, of course, I can't pay any more or have any bugs.
key_insight: You cannot compress a project's timeline without accepting tradeoffs in scope, resources, or quality — pretending otherwise doesn't change the laws of nature.
